verses.flow: In the eighth month, in the second year of Darius, the word of YHWH came to Zechariah the son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo the prophet, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The date formula places this oracle in October/November 520 BC, the same year Haggai began prophesying (Hag 1:1). Darius I (Hystaspes) ruled Persia 522–486 BC. The double patronymic (son of Berechiah, son of Iddo) may distinguish this Zechariah from others; Ezra 5:1 and 6:14 call him simply son of Iddo, perhaps using "son" loosely for "descendant." HEBREW: "Iddo" (Iddo) may mean "his appointed time," and "Berechiah" means "YHWH blesses," weaving covenantal meaning into the genealogy itself.

verses.flow: YHWH was very angry (qatsaph) with your fathers.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb qatsaph (Strong's H7107) denotes a burning, wrathful indignation — more intense than simple displeasure. The perfect tense frames YHWH's anger as a settled historical fact, the basis for the call to repentance that follows. This opening verse is deliberately blunt: the covenant breach was real and severe.

verses.flow: Therefore say to them: Thus says YHWH of Hosts (Tseva'ot): Return to me, declares YHWH of Hosts, and I will return to you, says YHWH of Hosts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The triple repetition of "YHWH of Hosts" (YHWH Tseva'ot) in a single verse is rhetorically emphatic, stressing the sovereign authority behind the covenant summons. The reciprocal structure — "return to me and I will return to you" — is classic covenant-renewal language echoing Mal 3:7 and Jer 3:22, grounded in the hesed (steadfast love) of the Mosaic covenant.

verses.flow: But my words and my statutes, which I commanded my servants the prophets, did they not overtake your fathers? And they turned and said: As YHWH of Hosts purposed to do to us, according to our ways and according to our deeds, so has he dealt with us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse marks the theological pivot of the prologue: the exiled generation finally acknowledged that YHWH's words had "overtaken" (nasag, H5381) them — a vivid term suggesting pursuit and capture, as of a fugitive. The confession is covenantally significant, echoing Lam 1:14 and Deut 28:15–68. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of divine word pursuing its fulfillment anticipates the NT theme that no word of God returns void (Isa 55:11), ultimately fulfilled in the incarnate Word (John 1:14).

verses.flow: On the twenty-fourth day of the eleventh month — that is, the month of Shebat — in the second year of Darius, the word of YHWH came to Zechariah the son of Berechiah, the son of Iddo the prophet, saying:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The twenty-fourth of Shebat corresponds to approximately February 15, 519 BC, three months after the initial call to repentance. Shebat is the Babylonian month name adopted into post-exilic Hebrew reckoning, itself a marker of the diaspora context. This date formula introduces the first of eight night visions (Zech 1:7–6:15), the most elaborate visionary sequence between Ezekiel and Daniel.

verses.flow: I saw in the night, and behold, a man riding upon a red horse, and he was standing among the myrtle trees that were in the ravine; and behind him were red, sorrel, and white horses.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rider among the myrtle trees presides over a patrol of heavenly horsemen — a divine reconnaissance corps surveying the nations of the earth. This imagery is closely parallel to the "sons of God" patrolling the earth in Job 1:7 and 2:2, and to the territorial watchers of Dan 10. The myrtle trees (hadas) in the ravine may symbolize Israel in its lowly, hidden post-exilic state. HEBREW: The three colors — red (adom), sorrel/speckled (saruq), and white (lavan) — recur in the sixth vision (Zech 6:2–3) with possible cosmic-symbolic significance in ANE color cosmology.

verses.flow: Then the angel of YHWH answered and said: O YHWH of Hosts, how long will you have no compassion (lo tachamel) for Jerusalem and for the cities of Judah, against which you have been indignant (za'amta) these seventy years?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Angel of YHWH intercedes before YHWH as a heavenly advocate — a mediatorial role anticipating Christ's intercessory work (Heb 7:25; Rom 8:34). The "seventy years" references Jeremiah's prophecy (Jer 25:11–12; 29:10) and frames the nations' continued dominance as a theological problem demanding divine resolution. HEBREW: Za'am (H2194, indignation) is stronger than simple anger — it carries the sense of divine curse-wrath, often associated with covenantal judgment.

verses.flow: So the angel who was speaking with me said to me: Cry out, saying: Thus says YHWH of Hosts — I am exceedingly jealous (qanne'ti qin'ah) for Jerusalem and for Zion.
verses.note: HEBREW: The expression qanne'ti qin'ah gedolah (I am jealous with great jealousy) is an emphatic cognate construction — literally "I have been jealous a great jealousy." The root qana (H7065) denotes the exclusive, possessive zeal of a husband for his wife or a lord for his covenant people, reflecting the marriage metaphor underlying the Sinai covenant. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's jealousy for Zion is the motivating force behind all the night visions — divine ownership of the holy city drives the eschatological program of the book.

verses.flow: And I am very angry with the nations that are at ease; for while I was only a little angry, they furthered the disaster.
Therefore thus says YHWH: I have returned to Jerusalem with compassion (rachamim); my house shall be built in it, declares YHWH of Hosts, and a measuring line shall be stretched out over Jerusalem.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The measuring line stretched over Jerusalem anticipates the ultimate eschatological restoration of the city, a theme developed fully in Zech 2:1–5 and ultimately in Rev 21:15–17 where the New Jerusalem is measured with a golden rod. The return of YHWH's compassion (rachamim, H7356 — lit. "womb-love," the deep mercy of a parent) signals covenant renewal after judgment.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Villages without walls" (peratzoth; H6519) signals an era of divine security surpassing human fortification. verses.flow: And I myself will be to her, declares YHWH, a wall of fire (chomah esh) all around, and for glory (kavod) I will be in her midst."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH himself as a "wall of fire" is unparalleled in the OT as a city-defense metaphor. The dual promise — protective fire without and kavod (glory; H3519) within — directly anticipates the New Jerusalem's temple-less state where God himself is its glory (Rev 21:22-23). TYPOLOGY: The kavod dwelling in the city's midst evokes the Shekinah cloud-pillar of the Exodus and points forward to the Incarnation, when the Word tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us (John 1:14).

verses.flow: For thus says YHWH of hosts: After glory (kavod) he has sent me to the nations who plundered you — for he who touches you touches the apple (babah) of his eye —
verses.note: HEBREW: "Apple of his eye" renders babah (H892), literally the "pupil" or "gate" of the eye — an idiom of extreme preciousness and vulnerability (cf. Deut 32:10; Ps 17:8). The identity of the divine speaker who is "sent" yet speaks with full divine authority creates a Christological tension: the Angel of YHWH who is both sent and is YHWH (cf. Mal 3:1). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The commissioning language ("he has sent me") reflects a divine council dispatch structure paralleled in Isaiah 6 and 1 Kings 22:19-22, where the heavenly court authorizes a messenger to act on earth.

verses.flow: "Sing and rejoice, O daughter of Zion, for behold, I am coming and I will dwell in your midst, declares YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The divine promise "I am coming and I will dwell in your midst" (shakhanti betokekh; from shakan, H7931) is the most direct pre-Incarnation promise of divine indwelling in the Twelve. The verb shakan is the root of Shekinah and the cognate of the Greek skēnē (tent/tabernacle). This verse stands as a direct antecedent to John 1:14 and Revelation 21:3. THEOLOGICAL: The address "daughter of Zion" (bat-Tziyon) is a covenant-community title used at pivotal moments of divine engagement (Zech 9:9; Mic 4:8; Zeph 3:14).

verses.flow: And many nations shall join themselves to YHWH in that day, and shall be to me a people, and I will dwell in your midst; and you shall know that YHWH of hosts has sent me to you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The ingathering of "many nations" (goyim rabbim) who become YHWH's people expands the covenant beyond ethnic Israel — a programmatic statement of eschatological universalism rooted in the Abrahamic promise that all nations would be blessed (Gen 12:3). The repeated phrase "you shall know that YHWH of hosts has sent me" functions as a divine-authentication formula, marking the fulfillment of the vision as proof of the sender's identity. TYPOLOGY: Paul cites this expansion of covenant membership as fulfilled in the Church (Eph 2:11-19; Rom 15:8-12).

verses.flow: And YHWH shall take possession of Judah as his portion (chelqo) in the holy land (adamah ha-qodesh), and shall again choose Jerusalem.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Holy land" (adamah ha-qodesh; H127 + H6944) is the only occurrence of this precise phrase in the Hebrew Bible, making it a unique designation. Adamah emphasizes the earthy, cultivated ground rather than merely territorial space (eretz). THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's "portion" (chelqo; H2506) inverts the Deuteronomy 32:8-9 divine allotment schema: just as the nations were allotted to the sons of God, Israel remains YHWH's own inheritance — here reaffirmed after exile as an act of re-election.

verses.flow: Be silent (has), all flesh, before YHWH, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling (me'on qodsho)."
verses.note: HEBREW: Has (H2013) is an onomatopoeic interjection of hushed silence — literally the sound of "shushing." It appears also in Hab 2:20 and Zeph 1:7 in identical theophanic contexts, marking the imminence of divine action. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The universal call to silence before YHWH as he "rouses himself from his holy dwelling" (me'on qodsho) is throne-room language, picturing the heavenly temple from which YHWH descends in judgment and salvation (cf. Ps 11:4; Isa 66:1; Mic 1:2-3). This verse forms the solemn doxological close of the vision.

verses.flow: And he showed me Joshua the high priest standing before the Angel of YHWH, and the Adversary (ha-Satan) standing at his right hand to accuse him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The scene depicts the heavenly court (divine council) in session, with "the Angel of YHWH" presiding as the divine representative and "ha-Satan" (הַשָּׂטָן, H7854) functioning as the prosecuting accuser — a juridical role, not yet the fully developed NT figure of the Devil, though the same being is in view. The definite article ("the Adversary") indicates a title or office, not a personal name. (Job 1:6; Rev 12:10)

verses.flow: And YHWH said to the Adversary, "YHWH rebuke you, O Adversary! Indeed, YHWH who chooses Jerusalem rebuke you! Is not this one a brand plucked from the fire?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's rebuke of the Adversary is grounded in the doctrine of divine election: "YHWH who chooses Jerusalem" (בֹּחֵר בִּירוּשָׁלָ͏ִם) asserts that the covenant community cannot be condemned when YHWH has sovereignly chosen them. The image of a "brand plucked from the fire" (אוּד מֻצָּל מֵאֵשׁ) echoes Amos 4:11 and signifies miraculous rescue from destruction — here likely referencing Judah's survival of the Babylonian exile. (Amos 4:11; Jude 23)

verses.flow: And he answered and spoke to those standing before him, saying, "Remove the filthy garments from upon him." And he said to him, "Behold, I have caused your iniquity to pass from upon you, and I will clothe you with festal robes."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The removal of filthy garments (בְּגָדִים צוֹאִים) and the investiture with festal robes (מַחֲלָצוֹת) is a powerful enacted parable of forensic justification — guilt removed and righteousness imputed. The Hebrew מַחֲלָצוֹת denotes costly or festal garments given as a token of honor, pointing forward to the "garments of salvation" and "robe of righteousness" of Isaiah 61:10 and ultimately to the righteousness of the Messiah credited to his people. (Isa 61:10; Rev 3:4–5; Rev 19:8)

verses.flow: And I said, "Let them set a clean turban upon his head." So they set the clean turban upon his head and clothed him with garments, while the Angel of YHWH was standing by.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "clean turban" (צָנִיף טָהוֹר, tsaniph tahor) placed on Joshua's head is priestly regalia (cf. Exod 28:4; 29:6), symbolizing his reinstatement to full high-priestly dignity. Notably, the prophet Zechariah himself interjects into the vision — "And I said" — demonstrating the participatory character of prophetic vision and the urgency of priestly restoration in the postexilic community. (Exod 28:4; Lev 8:9)

verses.flow: "Thus says YHWH of hosts: If you will walk in my ways and keep my charge, then you shall both govern my house and guard my courts, and I will give you access among these who are standing here.
"Hear now, O Joshua the high priest, you and your companions who sit before you — for they are men who are a sign — for behold, I am bringing my Servant, the Branch (Tsemach).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "My Servant, the Branch (Tsemach)" (צֶמַח, H6780) is one of the most direct messianic titles in the Hebrew Bible, also found in Zechariah 6:12, Isaiah 4:2, and Jeremiah 23:5; 33:15. Tsemach literally means "sprout" or "shoot," pointing to the Davidic king who would arise from the seemingly dead stump of the royal line after the exile. The NT identifies this figure unambiguously with Jesus of Nazareth, son of David. (Jer 23:5; Isa 11:1; Luke 1:32; Rev 22:16)

verses.flow: "For behold, upon the stone that I have set before Joshua — upon one stone with seven eyes — behold, I am engraving its inscription, declares YHWH of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of that land in a single day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "stone with seven eyes" is a richly layered symbol: the seven eyes (שִׁבְעָה עֵינַיִם) reappear in Zechariah 4:10 as the eyes of YHWH ranging through the earth, and in Revelation 5:6 as the seven eyes of the Lamb. In Second Temple Jewish interpretation, the stone often evokes the cornerstone of the Temple, but also the Messiah himself (Ps 118:22; Isa 28:16). The removal of iniquity "in a single day" (בְּיוֹם אֶחָד) anticipates the atoning work of the Messiah accomplished at Calvary. (Ps 118:22; Isa 28:16; Zech 4:10; Rev 5:6; John 19:30)

verses.flow: "In that day, declares YHWH of hosts, every man will call to his neighbor under the vine and under the fig tree."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The image of sitting "under the vine and under the fig tree" (תַּחַת גֶּפֶן וְתַחַת תְּאֵנָה) is a classic Hebrew idiom for Edenic peace, security, and covenant prosperity, found also in 1 Kings 4:25 and Micah 4:4. It frames the messianic age not as mere spiritual blessing but as the full restoration of shalom (שָׁלוֹם) — wholeness, harmony, and abundance — in the land and among the people. (1 Kgs 4:25; Mic 4:4; Rev 21:3–4)

verses.flow: And he said to me, "What do you see?" And I said, "I see, and behold, a lampstand (menorah) of gold in its entirety, with its bowl upon its top, and its seven lamps upon it — seven and seven pipes to the lamps that are upon its top."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Menorah" (Strong's H4501) is the seven-branched lampstand of the Tabernacle and Temple, derived from "ner" (lamp). The "bowl" (gullah, Strong's H1543) at the top functions as a central oil reservoir feeding all seven lamps. The "seven and seven" pipes (shiv'ah v'shiv'ah mutzaqot) indicates forty-nine channels, emphasizing the lavish, unimpeded flow of the divine supply of oil — a picture of the Spirit's inexhaustible provision. TYPOLOGY: The menorah is the primary symbol of Israel's priestly witness and the presence of God among His people (Exod 25:31-40). Its appearance here in a restored context anticipates the Lamb as the lamp of the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:23), and the seven lampstands representing the churches (Rev 1:12-20).

verses.flow: And he answered and said to me, "This is the word of YHWH to Zerubbabel, saying: Not by might (chayil), and not by power (koach), but by my Spirit (Ruach) — says YHWH of hosts (Tzevaot)."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chayil" (Strong's H2428) denotes military force or human strength; "koach" (Strong's H3581) denotes raw physical or natural power. The juxtaposition of both terms exhausts the category of human resource. "Ruach" (Strong's H7307) is the Spirit of God — the same creative and empowering Spirit of Gen 1:2. "YHWH Tzevaot" (LORD of hosts) invokes the divine warrior and sovereign of the heavenly armies. THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the thematic heart of the chapter. The rebuilding of the Temple — and by extension all covenant restoration — is not accomplished by human agency or political power but solely through the sovereign empowering of the divine Spirit. It directly counters the discouragement of the remnant community facing Persian opposition (Ezra 4-5).

verses.flow: "Who are you, O great mountain? Before Zerubbabel you shall become a plain! And he shall bring forth the capstone with shoutings of 'Grace (chen), grace upon it!'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "great mountain" likely symbolizes every obstacle — political, spiritual, and material — standing against the completion of the Temple. The capstone (even harosh, "the head stone") being brought forth with shouts of "chen, chen!" (grace, grace) is a declaration that the completion of the work is entirely an act of divine favor, not human merit. TYPOLOGY: The capstone imagery carries profound messianic resonance. Zerubbabel as a type of the greater Builder finds its antitype in Christ, the chief cornerstone (akrogoniaios) rejected by the builders but made the head of the corner (Ps 118:22; Matt 21:42; Eph 2:20).

verses.flow: "The hands of Zerubbabel have laid the foundation of this house, and his hands shall complete it. And you shall know that YHWH of hosts has sent me to you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Zerubbabel as the one whose hands both lay the foundation and complete the structure is a double type of Christ, who is simultaneously the foundation (1 Cor 3:11) and the one who will bring His building project to completion (Phil 1:6; Heb 12:2). The phrase "you shall know that YHWH of hosts has sent me" is a recurring authentication formula in Zechariah's visions, pointing to the divine origin of the prophetic word and foreshadowing the Son's own claim of being sent by the Father (John 17:3, 8).

verses.flow: "For who has despised the day of small things? For they shall rejoice and shall see the plumb stone in the hand of Zerubbabel — these seven are the eyes of YHWH, ranging to and fro through all the earth."
verses.note: HEBREW: The "seven eyes of YHWH" (shiv'at einei YHWH) correspond to the seven lamps of the menorah and echo Zech 3:9, where the "stone with seven eyes" is set before Joshua the high priest. These eyes "range to and fro" (shotim, from shut, to run about) through all the earth, expressing YHWH's omniscient providential governance. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The imagery of divine eyes surveying the entire earth connects to the heavenly patrol imagery of Zech 1:10-11 and Job 1-2, where divine agents report the condition of the earth to YHWH in His heavenly council. In Second Temple Judaism these offices were strictly separated; their union in one person was understood as an eschatological event. The shalom between priest and king speaks to the internal harmony of the Messiah's unified office, not merely a political alliance.

The slight variation in the name "Helem" (vs. "Heldai" in v. 10) and "Hen" (vs. "Josiah") may reflect honorific or alternative names, a practice well-attested in the Ancient Near East.

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH came to Zechariah, saying,

verses.note: HEBREW: Mishpat emet (מִשְׁפַּט אֱמֶת) is a construct chain meaning "judgment of truth" or "true justice," combining mishpat (justice, legal judgment; H4941) with emet (truth, faithfulness; H571). Hesed (חֶסֶד, H2617) is the covenant-loyalty love of YHWH and the pattern for human community ethics. Rachamim (רַחֲמִים) derives from rechem (womb), evoking a deep, visceral compassion. verses.flow: "For there shall be a sowing of peace (shalom): the vine shall give its fruit, and the ground shall give its produce, and the heavens shall give their dew, and I will cause the remnant of this people to possess all these things."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "sowing of shalom" reverses the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:38-42 and mirrors the restored creation order of Amos 9:13-15. Shalom (H7965) in this context is comprehensive well-being encompassing agricultural, social, and covenantal dimensions. The sequence — vine, ground, heavens, dew — moves from cultivated creation to the cosmic order, suggesting total restoration.

verses.flow: "And it shall come to pass that as you were a curse among the nations, O house of Judah and house of Israel, so I will save you, and you shall be a blessing. Fear not; let your hands be strong."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The reversal of Israel from "a curse among the nations" to "a blessing" fulfills the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 12:2-3. This verse looks forward to the inclusion of the Gentiles through the Messiah, who himself became a curse on behalf of his people (Gal 3:13-14) so that the blessing of Abraham might come upon all nations.

"These are the things that you shall do: Speak truth (emet), each man with his neighbor; render in your gates judgments that are true and make for shalom (shalom);"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The ethical demands of verses 16-17 ground eschatological hope in present covenant behavior. The pairing of emet (truth) with mishpat shalom (judgments of shalom) in the city gates — the site of ancient legal proceedings — reflects a vision of restorative, truth-oriented justice. Compare (Amos 5:15) and (Mic 6:8). The NT applies this ethic to the new covenant community (Eph 4:25).

verses.flow: And the word of YHWH of hosts came to me, saying,

"Thus says YHWH of hosts: The fast of the fourth month and the fast of the fifth and the fast of the seventh and the fast of the tenth shall become seasons of joy (sason) and gladness, and cheerful feasts for the house of Judah. Therefore love truth (emet) and shalom."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The four fasts commemorated disasters associated with Jerusalem's fall: the fourth month (Tammuz) marks the breach of Jerusalem's walls (2 Kgs 25:3-4); the fifth month (Av) marks the burning of the temple; the seventh month (Tishri) marks the assassination of Gedaliah (2 Kgs 25:25); the tenth month (Tevet) marks the beginning of Nebuchadnezzar's siege. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH promises their transformation into festivals of joy (sason, H8342), signaling the complete reversal of mourning — an eschatological motif echoed in (Isa 61:3) and (Rev 21:4).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's declaration "I will encamp at my house as a guard" signals a direct divine presence protecting the covenant community. verses.flow: Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout aloud, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your king is coming to you; righteous and having salvation is he, humble (ani) and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is directly cited in Matthew 21:5 and John 12:15 as fulfilled in the triumphal entry of Jesus into Jerusalem. The term "ani" (H6041, humble/afflicted) underscores the paradox of a righteous king who comes not in military conquest but in meekness. "Having salvation" (nosha') is a Niphal passive participle — the king is himself one who has been saved or vindicated by God, not merely one who saves. THEOLOGICAL: The dual mention of donkey and colt fulfills the parallelism of Hebrew poetry and was literally enacted by Jesus (Matt 21:2-7), demonstrating the precision of messianic prophecy.

TYPOLOGY: The dominion "from sea to sea, and from the River to the ends of the earth" echoes Psalm 72:8, linking the Messiah's reign to the Davidic covenant promise of universal kingship. This is fulfilled ultimately in the cosmic lordship of Jesus Christ (Phil 2:10-11; Rev 11:15). TYPOLOGY: The potter imagery may also echo Jer 18-19, linking judgment, sovereignty, and the remnant.

verses.flow: For behold, I am raising up a shepherd in the land who will not attend to the ones being cut off, nor will he seek the young, nor will he heal the one that is broken, nor will he sustain the one standing firm — but he will devour the flesh of the fat ones and tear off their hooves.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "worthless shepherd" raised up by YHWH represents a delegated but corrupt authority — a figure permitted within divine sovereignty as an instrument of judgment. This pattern (YHWH raising up a negative figure as discipline) is consistent with the divine council framework where rebellious or negligent delegated rulers are held accountable (cf. Ps 82). This figure has been interpreted as Antiochus IV Epiphanes, various apostate Jewish leaders, and eschatologically as a type of the Antichrist.

verses.flow: The burden (massa) of the word of YHWH concerning Israel: declares YHWH, who stretches out the heavens and lays the foundation of the earth and forms the spirit (ruach) of man within him —
verses.note: HEBREW: "Massa" (H4853, from nasa', "to lift/carry") is a technical term for a prophetic oracle of weighty or burdensome announcement, often of judgment. The triple participial construction — "stretches," "lays," "forms" — grounds the oracle in YHWH's absolute creative sovereignty over cosmos and the inner life of man. "Ruach" (H7307) here bridges cosmic creation (Gen 1:2) and the animating spirit poured on humanity (Gen 2:7), underscoring that YHWH who breathed life into man is the same one now acting in history.

verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The comparison of the house of David to "Elohim" and to "the angel (mal'ak) of YHWH" is extraordinary. verses.flow: And I will pour out upon the house of David and upon the inhabitants of Jerusalem a spirit (ruach) of grace (chen) and supplication (tachanun), so that they look upon me — him whom they have pierced (daqar). And they shall mourn for him as one mourns for an only child (yachid), and weep bitterly over him as one weeps bitterly over a firstborn.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is among the most theologically significant in the entire OT. The sudden shift from first person ("they look upon ME") to third person ("him whom they have pierced") within a single clause is unique and startling — YHWH identifies himself with the pierced one. John 19:37 explicitly cites this verse at the crucifixion of Jesus, identifying him as the one pierced. "Daqar" (H1856) means to thrust through, to pierce fatally. The mourning for an "only child" (yachid, H3173) echoes the Akedah of Isaac (Gen 22:2, 12, 16), the only other use of yachid in a father-son sacrificial context in the Torah. "Ruach of chen (grace) and tachanun (supplication)" anticipates the pouring out of the Spirit that produces corporate repentance — the spirit of mourning that regeneration brings. (John 19:37; Rev 1:7; Acts 2:37–38)

verses.flow: In that day the mourning in Jerusalem will be great, like the mourning of Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Hadad-rimmon" likely refers to the mourning rites associated with the death of the Canaanite storm god (Hadad/Baal) at Rimmon — a cultic mourning practice known across the ANE. Alternatively, it may refer to the historical mourning for King Josiah, who was killed at Megiddo (2 Kgs 23:29–30; 2 Chr 35:22–25). Either reading frames the mourning of v. 10 as the most intense imaginable — surpassing even the archetype of national lamentation.

verses.flow: And the land shall mourn, each family (mishpachah) by itself: the family of the house of David by itself, and their wives by themselves; the family of the house of Nathan by itself, and their wives by themselves;
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The four-fold repetition of "family by itself... wives by themselves" through vv. 12–14 creates a liturgical rhythm of comprehensive, individual accountability in mourning. Royal (David, Nathan), priestly (Levi), and Levitical subclans (Shimei) are each named — encompassing the totality of covenant community: kingship, prophethood, and priesthood all stand before the pierced one in broken repentance. This universality of mourning anticipates the NT theme of corporate confession before the crucified Messiah.

verses.flow: Awake, O sword (cherev), against my shepherd (ro'i), and against the man who is my associate (amit)," declares YHWH of hosts. "Strike the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered; and I will turn my hand against the little ones (ha-tse'irim).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is explicitly cited by Jesus on the night of his betrayal (Matt 26:31; Mark 14:27) as fulfilled in his arrest and the scattering of the Twelve. The designation of the shepherd as YHWH's "associate" (amit, H5997 — a near-equal companion, used in Leviticus of one's neighbor) is theologically remarkable: the one struck is no mere servant but one standing in intimate association with YHWH himself, implying a high Christology embedded in the Hebrew text. YHWH commands the sword against his own shepherd, underscoring the substitutionary, divinely-ordained nature of the Messiah's suffering. (Matt 26:31; Isa 53:10; John 10:11)

verses.flow: And his feet shall stand on that day upon the Mount of Olives, which is before Jerusalem on the east; and the Mount of Olives shall be split in two from east to west, making a very great valley; and half of the mountain shall move northward and half of it southward.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The theophanic descent of YHWH onto the Mount of Olives (har ha-zeitim) is the most geographically specific divine descent prophecy in the Hebrew canon. The splitting of the mountain recalls the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18) and anticipates the Ascension narrative, where Jesus departs from this very location (Acts 1:9-12), and the angelic promise of his return "in the same way" (Acts 1:11). TYPOLOGY: Early Christian interpreters identified this as the locus of Messiah's return, connecting it to the triumphal entry (Zech 9:9; Matt 21:1) and the passion (Matt 26:30).

verses.flow: Then everyone who survives of all the nations that came against Jerusalem shall go up year after year to worship the King, YHWH of hosts (Tzva'ot), and to keep the Feast of Booths (Sukkot).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The universalization of Sukkot (the Feast of Booths, or Tabernacles) as a required pilgrimage for "all the nations" transforms a distinctly Israelite feast into an eschatological festival of universal covenant inclusion. TYPOLOGY: Sukkot commemorated YHWH's provision in the wilderness and anticipated the ingathering harvest; its eschatological mandate here anticipates the NT's vision of the nations streaming to Zion (Isa 2:2-3; Rev 21:24-26) and the incarnation as God "tabernacling" (skenoo) among his people (John 1:14).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Qodesh le-YHWH" (Holy to YHWH) inscribed on horse bells was the very phrase engraved on the golden plate of the High Priest's turban (Exod 28:36-38), marking the holiest object in the sanctuary. Its transfer to common horse tack signals the eschatological collapse of the sacred-secular divide — all of creation becomes priestly and holy. STRUCTURE: This verse begins the climactic two-verse conclusion demonstrating that the holiness of the temple radiates outward to encompass the entire city and beyond.

verses.flow: And every pot in Jerusalem and in Judah shall be holy (qadosh) to YHWH of hosts, and all who sacrifice shall come and take of them and boil in them; and there shall no longer be a trader (Canaanite) in the house of YHWH of hosts on that day.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Canaanite" (kena'ani, H3669) functions as both an ethnic reference and a commercial term — "trader" or "merchant" (cf. Prov 31:24; Hos 12:7). The dual meaning is likely intentional: the eschatological temple will be purged of both pagan presence and commercial exploitation, echoing Jesus's cleansing of the temple (Matt 21:12-13; John 2:13-16). TYPOLOGY: John's temple cleansing is itself a messianic sign pointing to this Zecharian fulfillment, and the final removal of the "Canaanite" signals the completion of what the conquest began — total covenant holiness throughout the land.
